content.problemStatement:
**This is the hard version of the problem. Here you need to count the number of valid permutations PPP. In the easy version, you need to construct any valid permutation. This version is worth 505050 points in Division 111 and unscoreable in other Divisions.**

Stack had a permutation PPP of the integers [0,N‚àí1][0, N - 1][0,N‚àí1].

Based on that, he calculated an array AAA of length NNN as follows:


Ai=mex(P1,P2,...,Pi)A_i = \text{mex}(P_1, P_2, ..., P_i)Ai‚Äã=mex(P1‚Äã,P2‚Äã,...,Pi‚Äã) **OR** Ai=mex(Pi,Pi+1,...,PN)A_i = \text{mex}(P_i, P_{i + 1}, ..., P_N)Ai‚Äã=mex(Pi‚Äã,Pi+1‚Äã,...,PN‚Äã)
Now, Stack forgot the original permutation PPP.

In this version, he needs to **count the number of valid permutations** PPP that could have been the original permutation, given the array AAA.

Since the answer may be large, output it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line contains NNN - the size of the permutation.
The second line contains NNN elements - A1,A2,...,ANA_1, A_2, ..., A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,...,AN‚Äã.




Output Format
For each test case, count the number of valid permutations modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105
